audienceSections.normal: In Ana and Luis meeting as neighbors, you will use [[grammar:Soy + N]]. Use [[grammar:Soy]] before your name or role. Soy estudiante means ‘I am a student.’ Spanish can leave out yo because soy already shows who speaks; do not add an article before the simple profession role.
audienceSections.expert: This chapter presents [[grammar:Soy + N]] in Ana and Luis meeting as neighbors. Finite copula soy encodes first-person singular agreement and licenses a null subject. Bare profession predicates agree for grammatical gender where their form varies.

audienceSections.normal: Use [[grammar:Soy]] before your name or role. Soy estudiante means ‘I am a student.’ Spanish can leave out yo because soy already shows who speaks; do not add an article before the simple profession role.
audienceSections.expert: Finite copula soy encodes first-person singular agreement and licenses a null subject. Bare profession predicates agree for grammatical gender where their form varies.

audienceSections.normal: Read the Spanish punctuation with the sentence: ¿ starts a question and ¡ starts an exclamation. Learn nouns with el or la when an article is shown.
audienceSections.expert: The reading uses broadly intelligible contemporary Spanish with tú as neutral informal singular address. Null subjects and article-bearing citation forms are analyzed only where attested.

breakdown.normal:
Read each exact spanish line, then check its natural English meaning. Watch for [[grammar:Soy + N]] where it appears.

- ¡Hola! — Hello!
- Buenos días. — Good morning.
- Soy Ana. — I am Ana.
- Soy Luis, tu vecino. — I am Luis, your neighbor.
- Soy estudiante. — I am a student.
- Mucho gusto, soy cocinero. — Nice to meet you; I am a cook.
breakdown.expert:
These exact reading lines provide the distributional evidence for [[grammar:Soy + N]]. The grammar section above analyzes the relevant morphology and syntax.

- ¡Hola! — Hello!
- Buenos días. — Good morning.
- Soy Ana. — I am Ana.
